data.title: South African rand near five-month high on hopes of new inflation strategy
data.description:
South Africa's rand hovered near its strongest level in five months on Friday, a day after a minister said that a new inflation-targeting plan was imminent.

At 0833 GMT, the rand traded at 18.05 against the dollar , about 0.1% weaker than its previous close. It hit 17.9925 per dollar earlier in the session for the first time since mid-December.

Markets welcomed Deputy Finance Minister David Masondo's comments at an investor conference on Thursday that an announcement would be made "very soon" regarding South Africa's inflation-targeting regime.

The central bank's current inflation target is a 3-6% range and its governor Lesetja Kganyago has for years argued for a lower target.

"With little data on the economic front, markets' attention will likely remain on international geopolitics," said Andre Cilliers, currency strategist at TreasuryONE.

President Cyril Ramaphosa is scheduled to meet his U.S. counterpart Donald Trump next week in a bid to reset strained ties.

Ramaphosa's spokesperson Vincent Magwenya told the broadcaster SABC late on Thursday that the visit would be short and would not involve a large delegation.

On the stock market, the Top-40 index was little-changed.

South Africa's benchmark 2030 government bond (ZAR2030=) was stronger, with the yield down 5 basis points to 8.82%.

data.title: Bosnia’s first-time car registrations up 21% y/y in April
data.description:
SARAJEVO (Bosnia and Herzegovina), May 16 (SeeNews) - The number of first-time passenger car registrations in Bosnia and Herzegovina increased 21.2% year-on-year to 9,188 in April, the statistics office said.

The total number of motor vehicles registered in Bosnia in April rose 17.1% on the year, to 12,269, the statistics service said in a statement on Thursday.

In April, 1,118 of all first-time registered passenger cars were new, marking a 17.9% annual increase.

The largest number of newly registered vehicles in terms of fuel type was petrol (56.8%), followed by diesel (29.4%).

In 2024, Bosnia recorded 84,928 first-time passenger car registrations, a 23.8% increase over 2023.

data.title: Ukraine may lose 3.5 billion euros a year if EU trade privileges end, lawmaker says
data.description:
The end of preferential trade with the European Union in June could deprive Ukraine of 3.5 billion euros ($3.92 billion) in annual revenue, a hole other markets will be unable to fill, a senior Ukrainian lawmaker said on Friday.

The EU temporarily waived duties and quotas on Ukrainian agriculture after Russia's full-scale invasion and the measures are due to expire on June 5. EU diplomats said on Wednesday the bloc was weighing a return to its pre-war trade agreement with Ukraine while it negotiates a new deal with Kyiv.

The head of the parliamentary committee on finance issues Danylo Hetmantsev said on the Telegram messenger that corn, sugar, honey and poultry producers would be hit hardest.

"According to expert estimates, the new trade conditions following the end of the 'economic visa-free regime' could cost us 3.5 billion euro annually," Hetmantsev said.

He did not elaborate on the source of the estimates.

"This is a significant blow – in 2025, total monthly exports of goods averaged 2.9 billion euro, and it is practically impossible to compensate for such losses in agricultural exports in other markets," he said.

Ukraine's finance minister said on Wednesday he was in talks with Brussels to renew the emergency measures but the European Commission said no extension was planned.

EU farmers have repeatedly protested against a sudden influx of cheaper products from Ukraine and the Commission introduced "emergency brakes" on imports of poultry, sugar, oats, maize, groats, and honey if these exceed the yearly average in 2021-2023.

The Commission has also been eyeing a sharp cut to Ukrainian sugar imports amid complaints that large shipments have fuelled a collapse in prices.

($1 = 0.8923 euros)

data.title: Land Securities Stalled Growth Expected to Be Temporary — Market Talk
data.description: Although Land Securities' FY 2025 earnings are in line, the net asset value miss is slightly troubling, Quilter Cheviot analyst Oli Creasey says in a note. The London-listed commercial-property developer reported net tangible asset value per share of 874 pence, up from 859 pence the year prior, but below company-compiled consensus of 890 pence. Like-for-like rents rose 5% but earnings were largely unchanged, mostly due to property disposals, with nearly 500 million pounds sold in the year, Creasey says. Although analysts had expected the company to accelerate in the second half, growth stalled, he says. "The hope is that this is a temporary stutter rather than a false start," Creasey says. Shares are down 2.2% at 590 pence and have risen 1% in the year to date. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

data.title: Market Chatter: Japan, US Plan Third Round of Tariff Talks This Month After G7 Summit
data.description:
Japan and the US are arranging a third round of tariff negotiations later this month, with Japanese economic and fiscal policy minister Ryosei Akazawa set to visit the US after next week's G7 finance ministers and central bankers meeting in Canada, Nikkei Asia reported Friday.

Working-level discussions are expected to begin as early as next week, and cabinet-level talks will follow after US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent returns from the G7 summit, which runs through May 22. Talks will cover expanded bilateral trade, non-tariff barriers, and economic security cooperation.

Tokyo is preparing negotiation levers in all areas, including increased imports of US corn, soybeans, and possibly rice-though the latter has drawn opposition from Japan's farming sector, the report said.

On auto trade, Tokyo is resisting the idea of counting Japanese-brand vehicles made in the U.S. toward trade balance metrics, citing minimal benefit for Japanese automakers. However, it is considering easing current import rules by raising the cap on simplified certification for low-volume models, Nikkei noted.

Japan is also proposing joint efforts in shipbuilding-including investment, technology cooperation, and icebreaker development-as well as rare-earth metal security and stronger semiconductor supply chains, the report said.

The proposals aim to persuade Washington to roll back tariffs, including those on autos, Nikkei noted.

data.title: India's metal index set for biggest weekly gain in over 4 years
data.description:
** India's Nifty Metal Index gains 9.2% in the week so far, on track for biggest weekly gain since May 2021

** Base metal prices were on track to log weekly gains, supported by China-U.S. tariff truce, easing recession fears

** U.S. and China, earlier this week, said they have agreed on a 90-day pause on tariffs and reciprocal duties could drop sharply

** Index heavyweights, JSW Steel and Tata Steel gained 7% and 10% in the week; Jindal Steel was the top pct gainer, up 14.4%

** Most stocks on 15 member index rated "buy" on avg, per data compiled by LSEG

** .NIFTYMET down 0.2% on the day, same as benchmark Nifty 50

data.title: Cocoa Futures at Over 3-Month High
data.description:
Cocoa futures extended their rally, climbing toward $10,570 per tonne, nearing their highest level since early February, as concerns about crops in West Africa, particularly in Ivory Coast, continued to drive prices higher.

Dealers pointed to fears of a significant drop in the mid-season crop, with early pod count reports for the 2025/26 main crop suggesting limited prospects for a recovery in production next season.

Local farmers cautioned that recent rainfall has been insufficient, threatening yields unless heavier showers arrive in May and June to support the final phase of the April-to-September harvest.

data.title: Hannover Re's Results Were Misunderstood — Market Talk
data.description: Investors drew the wrong conclusions from Hannover Re's first-quarter print, Jefferies says in a research note following share weakness earlier this week. This wasn't a low-quality beat with currency gains offsetting softer underwriting, Philip Kett writes. Instead, the German reinsurer "opts to delay the emergence of one-off gains in earnings, trading it for balance sheet prudence that can smooth volatility later," he adds. This is a strategy management has used before, Kett adds. The long-term consequences of this delayed earnings recognition pattern is the accumulation of resilience reserves above the required levels, he adds. Shares recoup recent losses and trade up 1.7% at 280.8 euros. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0850 GMT - Investors drew the wrong conclusions from Hannover Re's first-quarter print, Jefferies says in a research note following share weakness earlier this week. This wasn't a low-quality beat with currency gains offsetting softer underwriting, Philip Kett writes. Instead, the German reinsurer "opts to delay the emergence of one-off gains in earnings, trading it for balance sheet prudence that can smooth volatility later," he adds. This is a strategy management has used before, Kett adds. The long-term consequences of this delayed earnings recognition pattern is the accumulation of resilience reserves above the required levels, he adds. Shares recoup recent losses and trade up 1.7% at 280.8 euros. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0827 GMT - The removal of the ring-fencing regime for U.K. banks could save around 1.5 billion pounds for the sector by scrapping operational, funding and other costs, RBC Capital Markets says in a research note. The CEOs of some of the country's top lenders wrote to the government asking it to abolish this regime, which requires banks with a certain level of core deposits to legally separate their retail banking activities from other operations. "We estimate that circa 160 billion pounds of excess liquidity is currently sitting in U.K. ring-fenced banks," analysts write, noting that this high quantity is likely due to several factors such as years of quantitative easing and higher savings since the pandemic. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0821 GMT - Standard Chartered's corporate and investment bank is in a good position within its geographical network, J.P. Morgan Cazenove says. The CIB has a strong leadership team that has refocused the unit on returns, operating leverage and risk-weighed asset optimization, JPM Cazenove says in a research note after attending a webinar with its management. "We came away constructive on growth opportunities including deeper wallet penetration with material income multipliers as clients utilize more products/markets, and targeted focus on network and financial institutional income," analysts write. The targets outlined for the unit broadly align with expectations. StanChart is well-placed as supply chains adjust, though its shares are exposed to volatility until tariff uncertainty clears, they add. Shares in London have risen 16% since the start of the year. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0816 GMT - Base metal prices fall in early trade, with LME three-month copper down 1.1% to $9,495.50 a metric ton and aluminum sliding 1.3% to $2,467 a ton. Despite the dip, both contracts are poised to end the week in positive territory, supported by easing fears of a tariff-induced recession after the U.S. and China reached a temporary trade deal. Risks on the supply side are also providing support, according to market watchers. "The recent increase in copper prices is driven by supply shortage concerns amid front-loading activities in the U.S. as the prospect of U.S. copper tariffs looms, as well as diminishing stock levels in Mainland China," say analysts at BMI, a unit of Fitch Solutions. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0812 GMT - Hensoldt would be the German defense company benefiting the most from Germany following the 5% of gross domestic product target for military spending, Citi analyst Charles Armitage says. German Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul said that the country is willing to commit to that target, demanded by U.S. President Donald Trump. NATO members are currently committed to spending at least 2% of GDP on defense, but this is expected to be increased at the next alliance summit in June. Hensoldt has the highest exposure to German defense with 58% of sales, considerably above Rheinmetall, Renk and Exosens, Armitage notes. Shares are up 2% at 73.50 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0807 GMT - Richemont's sector-leading growth at its jewelry division should reassure amid a volatile and complex environment, Stifel analyst Rogerio Fujimori says in a research note. The Swiss group reported sales of 5.17 billion euros for its fiscal fourth quarter ended March 31, with the core jewelry division growing at double digits. The watches segment recorded an 11% decline in quarterly sales at constant exchange rates, hit by weak demand in China. The company remains among the strongest market share winners in jewelry, which offsets ongoing challenges for watches, the analyst says. Shares climb 5%. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

0757 GMT - Standard Chartered's corporate and investment bank seems to be maintaining the good trajectory it has seen in the past five years, which was helped by leadership actions but also the period of rising rates and global volatility, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says. "It is often difficult to test management assertions... but it is clear that this business has outperformed in recent years," analysts Edward Firth and Elise Yu Ge write in a research note after a webinar with management. The business, which is key for the group as it brings in two-thirds of pretax profit, is betting on the deepening wallet share of large clients in a fragmented world for its next phase of growth. Shares in London have gained 16% year to date. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0752 GMT - Gold prices are on track for a weekly loss of nearly 4% as easing trade tensions between the U.S. and China weaken demand for safe-haven assets. In early trading, futures fall 0.3% to $3,216.80 a troy ounce, while the U.S. dollar trades 0.2% higher on the week, making bullion more expensive for holders of other currencies. "Prices have come down from the fresh record high of $3,500/oz amid the rollback of the U.S. and China's recently announced tariffs," analysts at BMI say. "Nevertheless, we continue to hold a bullish outlook." BMI forecasts prices in a range of between $3,000 and $3,400 a troy ounce in the second and third quarters, supported by U.S. policy uncertainty, geopolitical conflicts and inflation concerns. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0750 GMT - Hong Kong's IPO market has picked up this year, signaling a turnaround in the equity market, HSBC Global Research analysts write in a note. The amount raised by companies is more than double the total seen during the same period in 2024, they add. The brokerage expects funds raised through IPOs to reach a total of $17 billion this year--more than the amount raised in each of the past three years. Chinese firms raised more money in Hong Kong last year than on mainland exchanges, they note, and this trend is likely to continue to 2025. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0750 GMT - Commercial-property developer Land Securities' encouraging results confirm progress in leasing and efficiency, as well as recovery in asset values, Shore Capital analyst Andrew Saunders says. Although rental growth is improving the company is in the midst of a transformational overhaul. It is moving from being a London office and retail giant into a mixed-use regeneration specialist with strong residential credentials, supported by shopping centers and West End offices, he says. Still, there is a lot of work to do in implementing these changes and it will involve further capital recycling in the process, Saunders says. "Landsec's London portfolio looks in good shape and we're encouraged by the narrative on rental tone and early signs of recovery in the investment market," he says in a note. Shares are down 1.5% at 594.50 pence. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0750 GMT - Aegon's update feels a little messy overall but it won't likely move consensus expectations due to the one-off items, J.P.Morgan says in a note after the Dutch life insurer's first-quarter results. Analysts note the operating capital generation miss, the guidance retieration and the new 200 million-euro buyback, though it remains unclear whether there will be scope for another program this year. "The shares continue to look highly inexpensive to us; however, we are not sure if this update today will help close the valuation gap with peers," analysts write. Shares fall 1.2% to 6.14 euros. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0746 GMT - Aegon's figures didn't particularly excite but its share buyback looks moderately supportive, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says in a research note after the Dutch insurer's first-quarter print. The group announced a 200 million euro buyback that will run until the end of 2025, on top of its current 150 million program, which will be completed next month. "We feel that consensus was more optimistic for the year (300 million-400 million euro range we think)," analyst Michele Ballatore writes. The run-rate of its holding cash position, which management is guiding for a normalized level of around 1 billion euros for the year, leaves some flexibility, he adds. Shares are down 1.1% at 6.14 euros. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: Benzene prices drift lower in Europe
data.description: On Thursday, benzene prices journeyed southward in the European region.An industry source in Europe, on condition of anonymity, informed a Polymerupdate team member, "The price fall was prompted by bearish demand trends in the region. FOB Rotterdam benzene prices were assessed at the USD 695-705/mt levels, a drop of USD (-5/mt) from Wednesday.Downstream SM prices on Thursday were assessed at the USD 1280-1290/mt FOB Rotterdam levels, a day on day gain of USD (+10/mt).Meanwhile in Asia, benzene prices on Thursday were assessed at the USD 740-750/mt FOB Korea levels, down USD (-20/mt) from Wednesday's assessed levels.

data.title: Proact IT to Launch Share Buyback Program
data.description:
Proact IT Group's board plans to launch a share repurchase program on Nasdaq Stockholm to create a more flexible capital structure.

The Swedish data center and cloud company said Friday shares bought under the program will also be used for future acquisitions and long-term employee incentives.

Proact IT expects share repurchases to occur at any point up to the next annual general meeting.

data.title: Mitsui Chemicals to resume production at melamine unit
data.description: Mitsui Chemicals is likely to bring on stream its melamine unit in Takaishi.A Polymerupdate source in Japan informed a team member, "The company has planned to resume operations at the unit on May 20, 2025 following a turnaround. The unit was shut on March 20, 2025 for maintenance."Located in Takaishi, Japan, the unit has a production capacity of 40,000 mt/year.

data.title: STOXX 600 jumps as healthcare, earnings lift; set for weekly rise
data.description:
Key points:
  • Richemont sales growth beat expectations, shares up
  • Luxury index leads gains on STOXX 600
  • STOXX up 0.6%, DAX nears record high

European shares hit a seven-week high on Friday lifted by healthcare stocks and strong results from Cartier-owner Richemont, set to end the week higher as a U.S.-China truce supported sentiment.

The region-wide STOXX 600 index rose 0.6% as of 0833 GMT, set for its fifth consecutive weekly advance. Other local bourses also traded in the green at open, with Germany's hovering near a record high.

The heavyweight healthcare sub-index (.SXDP) jumped 1.7%, boosted by Novo Nordisk and Novartis .

Europe's luxury index (.STXLUXP) rose 2%, with Richemont rising 6.5%. The luxury group reported a slightly stronger-than-forecast 7% rise in quarterly sales, taking its shares 4% higher in early trading.

For the week, the European benchmark index rose 2.25% so far, with positive sentiment driven by a deal between Washington and Beijing to lower tariffs on each other which fueled optimism that a global recession could be averted.

A week ago, Trump and British Prime Minister Keir Starmer agreed on a limited bilateral trade agreement, making way for conversations that a deal with the European Union is also around the corner.

"There is a sort of renewed stability regarding the trade war and some more certainty for at least the coming time ahead helps stocks, but it's also fair to say that looking at stock markets now, it also seems that they're waiting for a new catalyst for further movements," said Teeuwe Mevissen, senior market economist at Rabobank.

The region was closely monitoring developments from the Russia-Ukraine peace talks, a day after Russian President Vladimir Putin declined to meet face-to-face with Ukraine's Volodymyr Zelenskiy.

Peace talks between the two warring are expected to take place in Turkey on Friday, as both sides came under pressure from the U.S. to end Europe's deadliest conflict since World War Two.

On the data front, French unemployment rate came at 7.1% for the first quarter.

Italian EU-harmonised consumer prices rose 2.0% year-on-year in April, revising down preliminary data. The preliminary estimate had pointed to a 2.1% year-on-year rise.

Investors awaited euro zone trade balance figures for March as well as Italy’s inflation rate, due later in the day.

European Central Bank policymaker Martins Kazaks said that interest rates may be at their lowest point but higher uncertainty and sudden changes could alter the policy outlook.

Among other stocks, Private equity firm CVC Capital Partners rose 3.1% after Morgan Stanley upgraded the stock to 'Overweight'.

Reinsurance company Swiss Re rose 2.1% after it posted a better-than-expected profit growth of 16% in the first quarter.

data.title: Dali Technology Ties Up With Autonomous Ship Manufacturer to Develop Smart Optoelectronic Systems for Unnamed Maritime Sector
data.description:
Zhejiang Dali Technology partnered with Zhuhai Yunzhou Intelligent Technology, an autonomous ship manufacturer, for the development of intelligent optoelectronic systems for the unmanned maritime sector, according to a Thursday disclosure on the Shenzhen bourse.

The Chinese infrared thermal imaging systems maker did not mention any price or timeline for the agreement, but it said that both parties agreed to establish a stable and long-term strategic partnership.

Dali Tech's shares closed less than 2% lower.

data.title: Transaction with Aker Property Group – Resolution to increase the share capital in relation to the tranche 1 consideration shares
data.description:
Reference is made to the stock exchange announcement made by Public Property Invest ASA ("PPI") on 13 May 2025, where PPI announced the transaction to acquire a portfolio of eight mission critical industrial infrastructure assets from TRG Real Estate AS ("TRG") in exchange for 124,398,074 new ordinary shares in PPI (the "Consideration Shares"). The Consideration Shares will be issued in three tranches.

To approve the issuance of the 30,524,657 Consideration Shares under tranche 1 (the "Tranche 1 Shares"), which will be issued subject to closing of the transaction, PPI's board of directors has today resolved to increase PPI's share capital with a total of NOK 1,526,232.85 pursuant to authorisations to increase PPI's share capital granted by PPI's general meeting on 12 April 2024.

Closing of the transaction is expected on or about 20 May 2025 and the Tranche 1 Shares will be issued shortly thereafter.

Of the 30,524,657 Tranche 1 Shares, 16,314,850 Tranche 1 Shares will upon issuance be delivered to SBB I Norden AB pursuant to the agreement between SBB I Norden AB, Samhällsbyggnadsbolaget i Norden AB and TRG referred to in the abovementioned stock exchange announcement.

This information is subject to disclosure under the Norwegian Securities Trading Act, §5-12.
